includedSources.name: The Dark Side Of The Boom / Stephen Innes

items.title: HFIR's oil war-premium calls expired worthless — right before the war got worse
items.idea: HFIR's July 17 Brent call options expired worthless; the author notes Brent stayed below $90/bbl even as the US and Iran returned to "all-out war." Written hours before other sources in this window (MacroEdge, Tiger Capital) report a 6th day of US strikes and Hormuz effectively closing — the war premium HFIR bet on may simply have arrived a weekend late.
items.angle: Cross-reference against MacroEdge and Tiger Capital Research below: both report fresh escalation (Yanbu strike, Hormuz near-zero transits) landing right after this trade expired. A timing/duration story, not a directional one.
items.tweetA: A trader lost money betting Brent would spike on US-Iran war. Then the war escalated for 6 more days and Hormuz shut. Being right on the catalyst and wrong on the expiry date is the most expensive mistake in oil options. $USO $XLE
items.tweetB: Brent stayed under $90 through "all-out war" — right until the Strait effectively closed days later. If your options expired Friday, you called the direction and missed the payout by a weekend. That's not a bad thesis, that's a duration problem.

charts.desc: Charts likely behind paywall — worth opening for the position detail.

items.source: The Dark Side Of The Boom / Stephen Innes
items.title: Goldman: global alignment is at its weakest since the 1980s — and fragmentation already cost 1% of GDP
items.idea: IMF: severe geoeconomic fragmentation could eventually cost up to 7% of global GDP. Goldman (Johan Allen, Joseph Briggs): fragmentation since 2016 has already cut global GDP by roughly 1%, with EM absorbing a much heavier hit than developed markets. Immediate Iran-conflict risk is fading, but broader alignment metrics are at 1980s lows.
items.angle: This is the slow-bleed thesis underneath every ceasefire headline — a structurally higher risk premium and lower trend growth, not a one-off shock. Relevant to EM vs DM allocation and gold's structural bid.
items.tweetA: Goldman says global political alignment is at its worst since the 1980s. The IMF's worst case for fragmentation: -7% of global GDP. That's not a shock, that's a permanent tax nobody voted for, paid disproportionately by EM.
items.tweetB: Everyone's watching the Iran ceasefire clock. The actual multi-year story is that trade, capital and tech are being re-sorted by "whose side are you on," not price. Fragmentation has already cost 1% of global GDP since 2016 — before any of this year's escalation.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening (Goldman research notes typically include the alignment-index chart).

items.title: Kimi K3: a 2.8T-parameter open-weights model just landed within reach of Opus 4.8 — at a third of the price
items.idea: Moonshot AI's Kimi K3 (2.8T parameters, open-weights) lands close to Anthropic's Opus 4.8 in quality at roughly a third of the price or less. Parekh's take: Anthropic and OpenAI would likely have been down 10%+ on the week if already public.
items.angle: Open-weight Chinese frontier models are becoming a real ceiling on closed-lab pricing power. The moat argument for foundation labs gets weaker every quarter this keeps happening.
items.tweetA: A 2.8 trillion parameter open-weights model just landed within shouting distance of Opus 4.8 — at a third of the price, fully inspectable. If Anthropic and OpenAI were public, this week's price action on the labs would've been ugly. The moat was never the model.
items.tweetB: The real trade in AI isn't "US vs China leads." It's that US enterprises now get Chinese open-weight frontier models as a standing pressure valve on closed-lab pricing. Kimi K3 doesn't need to win — it just needs to exist as the next cheaper option.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the Kimi K3 vs Opus 4.8 benchmark comparisons.

items.title: Gemini 3.5 Pro missed its ship date — specifically in the AI coding race
items.idea: Google's Gemini 3.5 Pro is reportedly behind schedule by months, with the slip landing specifically in AI coding — the layer where Anthropic's Fable 5 and OpenAI's GPT-5.6 are currently surging. This is Google's third "Code Red" reversal in the current AI cycle.
items.angle: A sentiment indicator on GOOGL more than a fundamental verdict — Google has reversed this exact narrative twice already in two years.
items.tweetA: Gemini's flagship model missed its internal ship date specifically in coding — the exact layer where enterprise AI spend is concentrating right now. Third "Code Red" reversal in two years. Google keeps proving it can catch up; the market keeps forgetting that.
items.tweetB: Nobody prices in that Google's AI narrative has already whipsawed twice — behind, then ahead, now behind again. If you're trading GOOGL on this week's AI headline, you're trading noise in a cycle that resets every few months.

items.title: Apple sues OpenAI, ships a chip roadmap, and cuts AI deals with Alibaba and Baidu — same week
items.idea: Apple filed a trade-secrets lawsuit against OpenAI over AI devices (400+ Jony Ive-era hires implicated), just ahead of OpenAI's expected mega-IPO. Simultaneously: an updated chip roadmap (M6, M7 Pro/Max/Ultra into M8) for on-device/server Apple Intelligence, and partnerships with Alibaba (Qwen) and Baidu to power Apple Intelligence/Siri in China.
items.angle: Three separate AI wars being run by one company at once — legal offense against a soon-to-be-public rival, silicon self-sufficiency, and localized partnerships to route around US-China friction.
items.tweetA: Apple filed a trade-secrets suit against OpenAI weeks before OpenAI's expected IPO — while simultaneously cutting AI deals with Alibaba and Baidu to stay legal in China. Same company, opposite geopolitics, same week. That's not confusion, that's optionality.
items.tweetB: Apple doesn't need to win the frontier model race. It needs Siri to work in China (Baidu/Alibaba), on-device to work everywhere (its own silicon roadmap through M8), and OpenAI's device ambitions to arrive pre-damaged by litigation. Three different AI wars, one company.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the chip roadmap timeline.

items.title: Nvidia's "kingmaker" strategy: arming open-source labs with Vera Rubin chips before anyone else gets them
items.idea: Nvidia backed Thinking Machines' (ex-OpenAI talent, led by Mira Murati) first open-source model "Inkling," providing the company with its latest Vera Rubin chips to train it. This is Nvidia's second such bet after Reflection AI — a pattern of seeding a stable of US open-source champions with capital and silicon.
items.angle: A vendor buying its own demand curve — Nvidia gets a seat at the table of every lab it arms as a US counterweight to Kimi K3/DeepSeek/Qwen, without building a model itself.
items.tweetA: Nvidia isn't just selling chips to labs anymore, it's seeding them. Two bets now — Reflection AI, now Thinking Machines — get Nvidia's newest Vera Rubin silicon before almost anyone else. That's a vendor buying its own demand curve.
items.tweetB: The "kingmaker" pattern matters more than any single model release: Nvidia gets leverage over every lab it arms, plus a US open-source counterweight to DeepSeek and Kimi, without having to build a model itself. It's the ultimate picks-and-shovels hedge.

items.title: DeepSeek is reportedly raising again — weeks after its last round — at $70B+
items.idea: China's DeepSeek is potentially back for a second major funding round at a valuation above $70 billion, only a month after already raising billions. Parekh frames it alongside Kimi K3 and Qwen as evidence Chinese open-model players are surging together, with Asian markets hitting new highs alongside them.
items.angle: The velocity of the raise is the real story — pricing keeps moving up even as the US narrative argues China is capital- or compute-constrained.
items.tweetA: DeepSeek is reportedly back raising again — weeks after its last round — at a valuation north of $70B. Whatever gap people think exists between US and Chinese frontier labs, the money is pricing it as closing, not widening.
items.tweetB: Compare the cadence: DeepSeek's second raise inside a month vs. the usual 12-18 month funding cycle for a frontier lab. Either investors know something about upcoming releases, or "China is capital constrained on AI" just stopped being true.

items.title: IRGC hit Yanbu directly — that's a strike on Saudi soil, not a tanker skirmish, and crack spreads just hit a record
items.idea: In the last 6 hours, the IRGC targeted Yanbu directly — a notable escalation beyond the tanker harassment that's defined the last 36-48 hours. The Strait of Hormuz is back near zero transits. The 3-2-1 crack spread hit a record ~$70, diesel is over $5/gal nationwide, and rig counts popped w/w. MacroEdge's framework: Iran needed Brent at ~$70 to shut the Strait and needs it at ~$85+ to sustain the fight — the binary for the weekend is "escalate or inflate."
items.angle: This is the connective tissue between the war and August CPI — a direct strike on Saudi infrastructure is a different order of escalation than tanker harassment, and record crack spreads mean it's already at the pump, not just the futures curve.
items.tweetA: IRGC hit Yanbu directly in the last 6 hours. That's not a tanker skirmish, that's a strike on Saudi soil. The 3-2-1 crack spread just hit a record ~$70 and diesel is over $5 nationwide — this is already in consumer prices, not just Brent futures.
items.tweetB: Iran's breakeven on this war is roughly $85 Brent — MacroEdge's framework, and it explains everything. They don't need Brent at $150, they need it high enough to fund the fight indefinitely. "Escalate or inflate" is the right binary for August CPI, not "ceasefire or not."

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the crack spread and rig count charts.

items.title: 1 in 30 Korean adults got margin called in the recent equity drawdown
items.idea: MacroEdge notes the recent violent drawdown in Asian equities saw 1 in 30 Korean adults margin called in a short period, while the KOSPI's holiday briefly spared Korean traders a further rapid unwind.
items.angle: A staggering household-leverage stat that reframes the KRW's recent strength (see item below): the currency isn't rallying on fundamentals, it's reflecting forced deleveraging.
items.tweetA: 1 in 30 Korean adults got margin called in this drawdown. Not 1 in 30 traders — 1 in 30 adults. That's the kind of retail leverage number that explains why Korean equities can't just "bounce back" — the forced sellers aren't done.
items.tweetB: Everyone's asking why the won strengthened while KOSPI fell — the answer is margin calls, not fundamentals. When 1 in 30 adults gets force-liquidated, deleveraging looks like currency strength for a while. It's not a bid, it's an unwind.

items.source: The Dark Side Of The Boom / Stephen Innes
items.title: FX carry is back in charge — and the dollar's edge is that it's both the shelter AND the yield trade
items.idea: Carry dominates FX while volatility stays contained and rate dispersion stays wide. The dollar's structural advantage: it combines defensive/reserve demand with one of the highest yields in the developed-market complex — a combination the author calls "cross-asset insurance" rather than a crisis trade.
items.angle: The same "quiet accumulation despite headline stress" pattern shows up in bitcoin's ETF flows this window (see crypto items below) — multiple asset classes are pricing "contained stress" right as the Yanbu strike raises the odds that assumption breaks.
items.tweetA: FX is trading carry, not crisis — same as bitcoin's ETF flows this week. Every asset class right now is being priced for "contained stress." The Yanbu strike is the first data point that could break that assumption across all of them at once.
items.tweetB: The dollar's edge right now isn't a story, it's math: it's simultaneously the defensive shelter AND one of the highest-yielding DM currencies. Betting against that combination isn't a trade, it's a subsidy to whoever's on the other side.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the carry-model banner/visual.

items.source: The Dark Side Of The Boom / Stephen Innes
items.title: Sterling ran on a short squeeze, not on rate differentials — fade above 1.3250, confirm above 1.3600
items.idea: Sterling's July rally has moved ahead of its rate and cyclical fundamentals — driven by shifting political expectations, technical buying, and forced unwinding of stale EUR/GBP shorts, not by rate differentials actually moving in the pound's favor. Tactical preference: fade toward 1.3250, with a break above 1.3600 signaling the market found "another gear."
items.tweetA: Sterling ran on political optimism and a EUR/GBP short squeeze, not on rate differentials moving in its favor. Optimism isn't cash flow — the market eventually asks for receipts. 1.3250 is the retracement level if it doesn't get them. $GBPUSD
items.tweetB: The setup here is clean: GBP rallied on flow (forced short-covering), not fundamentals (rates didn't move). That's the textbook signature of a fade, not a trend — the question is just whether 1.3600 breaks first and proves the flow was actually a new regime.

items.source: The Dark Side Of The Boom / Stephen Innes
items.title: The won's July bounce is a relief rally wearing a new-regime costume
items.idea: The Korean won strengthened even as Korean equities sold off — normally they move together. The likely explanation: reduced forced-selling/leverage (tighter speculative-position and margin rules) rather than genuine capital inflow. Even on Korea's best equity days this month, the won often failed to benefit — the tell that nothing structural has changed yet.
items.angle: Read together with MacroEdge's "1 in 30 Korean adults margin called" stat above — the won's strength is very plausibly the deleveraging unwind, not fresh buying.
items.tweetA: The won strengthening while KOSPI craters looks like decoupling. It's not — it's forced deleveraging reducing outbound hedging flows. Until Korean households actually change how much they send overseas, this is a relief rally wearing a new-regime costume.
items.tweetB: Watch what didn't happen: Korean equities had their best days this month and the won still didn't benefit. That's the tell that this isn't a currency finding a bottom, it's a currency temporarily short outbound flow because forced sellers are busy getting liquidated.

items.source: The Dark Side Of The Boom / Stephen Innes
items.title: The Swiss franc quietly took the yen's old job as the market's favorite funding currency
items.idea: CHF is becoming the market's preferred G10 funding currency: low inflation, little reason for the SNB to hike, wide policy differentials, and compressed cross-vol. Crucially, the franc's traditional link to gold has weakened, so it no longer spikes every time investors get nervous — making it more dependable to fund through. Main risk: a renewed, sustained gold rally reviving the haven bid.
items.tweetA: The franc just quietly took the yen's old job. Cheap to borrow, wide rate gap, and — the underrated part — its historic link to gold has weakened, so it doesn't spike every time investors get nervous anymore. That's what makes it fundable, not just cheap.
items.tweetB: If you want to know when the CHF-as-funder trade breaks, don't watch Swiss data — watch gold. A real bullion rally is the only thing that reconnects the franc to its old haven behavior and blows up every carry short funded through it.

items.source: The Dark Side Of The Boom / Stephen Innes
items.title: The baht is a mechanical gold proxy, not a vibes-based one — and that's now a headwind
items.idea: Thailand is a major regional gold-trading hub, so bullion purchases/sales generate real currency flows through domestic dealers — a mechanical relationship, not sentiment. Gold's slide this year turned that tailwind into a headwind, compounded by negative Thai real rates (1% policy rate against rising inflation) just as US real yields rise. Short THB/INR is the preferred expression.
items.angle: A genuinely under-discussed FX-commodity plumbing mechanism worth flagging on its own — most FX commentary treats "gold-linked currency" as folklore, not literal dealer flow.
items.tweetA: Most people think "gold-linked currency" is vibes. The baht is literally mechanical: Thailand is a regional gold-trading hub, so bullion selling generates real dollar demand through domestic dealers. Gold's slide this year didn't just hurt sentiment, it hurt the currency's plumbing.
items.tweetB: Thai real rates are negative — 1% policy rate against rising inflation — at the exact moment US real yields are rising. Combine that with the gold-flow mechanism breaking down and "short THB/INR" isn't a view, it's just two structural forces pointing the same way.

items.source: The Dark Side Of The Boom / Stephen Innes
items.title: AUD/NZD: the trade rotated from a commodity story to a rate-differential story
items.idea: The author took profit on an initial AUD/NZD short at 1.2250 and has re-entered via six-month 1.1650 puts. Thesis: RBNZ tightening risk is rising while RBA hike expectations lose conviction — and China isn't delivering supercycle-level stimulus, so the cross is starting to respond more to relative rates than to commodity prices.
items.tweetA: A trader banked profits on an AUD/NZD short at 1.2250 and immediately re-loaded the same view through 6-month 1.1650 puts instead of spot. That's not conviction, that's someone who thinks the move isn't over but wants defined risk on the next leg.
items.tweetB: The AUD/NZD story used to be about commodities. Now it's about who hikes next — New Zealand's the one with tightening risk, Australia's the one that's plateaued. When a cross stops reacting to commodity prices and starts reacting to rate differentials, that's the regime change.

items.title: June's soft CPI drove this week's rally. July's print will reflect 6 days of strikes and a shut Hormuz.
items.idea: A sixth consecutive day of US airstrikes against Iran; the Strait of Hormuz remains "effectively closed." June's CPI (-0.4% MoM, biggest drop since April 2020) took July rate-hike odds from above 40% to 10-13% and sparked this week's risk rally — but that data was captured during a window when tensions looked like they were easing. They weren't.
items.angle: A timing trap the market hasn't repriced yet: every soft-landing data point from the last month reflects a world that no longer exists by the time July's print lands.
items.tweetA: June's CPI print took July rate-hike odds from 40% to 10-13% and sparked this week's risk rally. That data was collected before Hormuz shut and before 6 straight days of US strikes on Iran. The July print isn't going to look anything like June's — and the market hasn't repriced for that yet.
items.tweetB: There's a real timing trap here: every "soft landing" data point from the last month was captured during the window when the war looked like it was cooling. It wasn't. Six days of strikes later, whatever July's inflation print shows, it's measuring a different world than June's did.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the CPI/hike-odds chart.

items.title: Bitcoin's correlation to equities just hit a record 0.96 — the AI selloff is now bitcoin's selloff
items.idea: A deepening global chipmaker selloff dragged BTC below $63,000, erasing the week's $65,000 breakout that soft CPI data had produced. Bitcoin's correlation with equities hit a record 0.96. The framing used: "AI frenzy losing steam leaves bitcoin less volatile than South Korean stocks."
items.angle: That correlation number kills the crypto-as-diversifier pitch outright — right now, BTC's risk profile is set by Nvidia's earnings calendar, not by anything crypto-native.
items.tweetA: Bitcoin's correlation to equities just hit a record 0.96. That number alone kills the "crypto as diversifier" pitch — right now BTC IS the Nasdaq, just with worse liquidity on the way down.
items.tweetB: Someone will tell you bitcoin is "less volatile than South Korean stocks" right now like it's a bullish maturity signal. It's not — it means BTC stopped being the highest-beta asset in the room because the AI trade took that title. That's a regime change in what crypto even is right now, not a compliment.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the BTC/Nasdaq correlation chart.

items.title: $368M into BTC ETFs, $28M OUT of ETH ETFs, same week — the ETH-leadership thesis just lost its flow support
items.idea: US spot BTC ETFs pulled in $79.2M Thursday, extending a three-day streak to $368M, broadening across IBIT, FBTC and BITB. Spot Ether ETFs lost $28M the same day, ending their inflow streak; Solana ETFs gained just $1.7M. ETH fell 2.8% vs BTC's 1.4% the same session — ETH underperforming BTC for the first time in two weeks.
items.angle: The ETH-leads-the-recovery thesis was always a flow argument ("its ETF complex outdraws bitcoin's"), not a price argument — that foundation broke on a single Thursday.
items.tweetA: $368M into BTC ETFs over three days, broadening across all three major issuers. $28M OUT of ETH ETFs the same week, ending its streak. If you're still holding the "ETH leads the next leg" thesis, the flows just voted against you.
items.tweetB: The ETH-leadership case was never really a price thesis, it was a flow thesis — "its ETF complex will outdraw bitcoin's." That thesis just broke on a single Thursday. Doesn't mean ETH is wrong long-term, it means whoever's trading the next two weeks should be positioned in BTC, not ETH.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the daily ETF flow chart.

items.title: Solana just passed Ethereum in real-world-asset holder count — 300,130 holders
items.idea: Solana became the largest blockchain by RWA holder count (300,130), moving ahead of Plume, Ethereum and BNB Chain — even as its price remains stuck below $80 and its ETF drew just $1.7M.
items.angle: Fundamentals (holder count) and flows (ETF demand) are telling opposite stories on SOL right now — a divergence that historically resolves once one side catches up to the other.
items.tweetA: Solana just passed Ethereum in real-world-asset holder count — 300,130 holders, the actual chain where RWA adoption is happening isn't the one everyone assumed. Meanwhile its ETF pulled in $1.7M. Fundamentals and flows are telling two completely different stories right now.
items.tweetB: The RWA narrative has been "Ethereum's killer app" for two years. Solana just quietly took the holder-count lead. Price hasn't caught up yet — still can't clear $80 — which is either the market being slow or the market knowing something about holder count that doesn't equal revenue.

items.title: Morgan Stanley's E*TRADE just started a crypto fee war against Coinbase, Robinhood and Schwab
items.idea: Morgan Stanley's E*TRADE opened spot BTC/ETH/SOL trading to its 8.6M customers at 50bps via Zerohash — undercutting Coinbase, Robinhood and Schwab, which charge 60-95bps.
items.angle: Distribution-plus-trust was Coinbase's moat; a bank just took the distribution half for free by using an existing brokerage customer base.
items.tweetA: Morgan Stanley just opened spot crypto to 8.6 million E*TRADE customers at 50bps — undercutting Coinbase, Robinhood and Schwab by 10-45bps in one move. That's not a product launch, that's a fee war started by a bank against the crypto-native platforms.
items.tweetB: Retail crypto access used to mean choosing an exchange. Now it means comparing basis points across brokerages that already had your account. Coinbase's moat was distribution-plus-trust; Morgan Stanley just took the distribution part for free.

items.title: Japan's parliament just reclassified crypto as a "financial instrument" — spot ETFs and a lower flat tax now realistic
items.idea: Japan's parliamentary committee approved a bill reclassifying digital assets as financial instruments, opening a path to spot crypto ETFs and a lower flat tax rate.
items.angle: One of the world's largest retail savings pools just got a regulatory green light nobody in Western crypto discourse is pricing in yet.
items.tweetA: Japan just moved crypto into the "financial instrument" bucket legally — meaning spot ETFs and a flat tax rate become realistic instead of theoretical. That's one of the world's largest retail savings pools getting a regulatory green light nobody's pricing in yet.
items.tweetB: The US argues about market-structure bills for years at a time. Japan just reclassified the entire asset class in a committee vote. Regulatory clarity isn't a US monopoly — it's increasingly an Asia story, and capital tends to follow clarity.

items.title: Bitcoin fell 34% in a half-year where risk assets broadly had a good run
items.idea: BTC fell over 34% in H1 2026, touching a 2026 low of $57,800 on July 1 versus a yearly open of $87,648. Net ETF outflows for the year total roughly 120,000 BTC, with AUM down from ~$104B to ~$94B — institutions remain net sellers on the year even after July's inflow streak.
items.angle: The 3-day $368M inflow streak (see item above) is real but small relative to the year's outflow — a good week offsetting roughly three days of a bad half.
items.tweetA: Bitcoin fell 34% in a half-year where risk assets broadly had a good run. That's not "crypto is risk-on beta," that's crypto underperforming its own supposed asset class. 120,000 BTC of net ETF outflows year-to-date says institutions agree.
items.tweetB: Everyone's excited about the three-day BTC ETF inflow streak. Zoom out: AUM is still down from $104B to $94B this year. A good week doesn't offset a bad half — it offsets about three days of it.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the H1 BTC drawdown chart.

items.source: The Dark Side Of The Boom / Stephen Innes
items.title: Goldman: record-low stock correlations mean capital is rotating out of AI/Momentum, not fleeing equities
items.idea: Goldman's Ben Snider (with Ryan Hammond, Jenny Ma, Daniel Chavez, Kartik Jayachandran, Christophe Sung) names three themes outside the AI/Momentum pressure cooker: consumer experience stocks, high-quality compounders, and M&A candidates. Record-low stock correlations signal rotation, not an exit. Only modest capex-estimate revisions are expected this earnings season, after last quarter's ~$100B upward revision — the market wants an earnings "bridge" that may not arrive yet.
items.tweetA: Stock correlations just hit a record low. That's not chaos, that's capital finally finding somewhere else to go besides the AI trade — Goldman's naming consumer experience names, quality compounders, and M&A targets as the beneficiaries.
items.tweetB: The market wants hyperscaler earnings to prove the AI capex cycle is paying off. Goldman's own math says don't expect much — only modest estimate revisions this quarter after already pricing in a $100B raise last quarter. The "bridge" everyone's waiting for might not show up until 2027 guidance season.

charts.desc: Charts inside — worth opening for the stock-correlation chart.
